## 3. Google (Gemini 1.5/2.0/2.5)

### 3.1 Long Context Architecture: What's Actually Known

**From the Gemini 1.5 technical report (arxiv: 2403.05530):**
Gemini 1.5 Pro is a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformer. The paper claims "near-perfect recall (>99%) up to at least 10M tokens" and "100% recall at 530K tokens, 99.7% at 1M tokens." However, the paper is deliberately vague on implementation specifics — it describes the results but not the mechanisms that achieve them.

What is disclosed: "a series of significant architecture changes" enable long-context understanding. The report does not publicly specify whether this involves ring attention, linear attention, or sparse attention variants. Google treats the specific architectural mechanisms as proprietary.

**Inference infrastructure (from arxiv: 2411.01783 — "Context Parallelism for Scalable Million-Token Inference"):**
For serving long-context requests, Google uses **context parallelism (CP)** — distributing the KV cache and attention computation across multiple GPUs in a ring pattern:

- **Pass-KV algorithm:** Distributes key-value tensors across ranks in a ring. Each rank holds a chunk of the sequence; KV blocks circulate around the ring while each rank computes partial attention results. Most efficient for full-prefill scenarios.
- **Pass-Q algorithm:** Query embeddings circulate instead of KV. Preferred during decode and in scenarios with high prefix cache hit rates (when cached context far exceeds new tokens). The switching threshold is determined by: `T/(T+P) ≤ 2·(NKV/NH)` where T = new tokens, P = cached tokens, NKV = KV heads, NH = query heads.
- **Dynamic selection heuristic:** The system automatically selects between Pass-KV and Pass-Q based on the ratio of new to total tokens.
- **Scaling results:** Achieved 1M-token prefill with Llama3 405B in 77 seconds on 128 H100s (93% parallelization efficiency); 128K context in 3.8 seconds.

**This is a distributed inference strategy, not a compression mechanism.** The full context is processed without approximation — lossless, with near-linear latency scaling.

### 3.2 Context Caching

Google's context caching is the primary developer-facing long-context management tool.

**Explicit caching:**
- Developer sends a cacheable prefix (system prompt, large document, video, audio) to the `/cachedContents` API
- Google stores the prefix as a named cache object with a configurable TTL (default: 1 hour)
- Subsequent requests reference the cache object; the cached tokens are processed at 75% discount (90% on Gemini 2.5)
- Minimum token thresholds: 1,024 tokens (Gemini 2.5 Flash), 4,096 tokens (Gemini 2.5 Pro)
- The cached content functions strictly as a **prefix** to the prompt; it cannot appear mid-conversation
- Storage costs billed by TTL duration × cached token count

**Implicit caching (Gemini 2.5+, launched May 2025):**
- Enabled by default for all Gemini 2.5+ models — no developer action required
- The API automatically identifies common prefixes across requests and applies cache discounts
- 90% discount on Gemini 2.5 models (versus 75% on 2.0)
- Minimum: 2,048 tokens for Gemini 2.5 Pro, 1,024 for 2.5 Flash
- The `usage_metadata` field returns `cached_content_token_count` for transparency
- Unlike Anthropic's prompt caching, there is no developer-marked breakpoint — the infrastructure infers the common prefix automatically

**This is not context compression.** Like Anthropic's prompt caching, it reduces redundant computation (reusing KV tensors), not the amount of context the model processes.

### 3.3 No Application-Level Truncation or Summarization

Gemini's API does not offer any equivalent to Anthropic's Compaction API or OpenAI's Assistants API truncation. Google's explicit strategy is: **"skip the RAG workflows with a 2M context window"** — the context window is large enough that summarization and chunking are positioned as unnecessary. When a context exceeds the window, the API returns a context-too-long error; there is no automatic fallback or graceful truncation.

### 3.4 Google AI Studio vs. Gemini API

Google AI Studio (the consumer/developer UI) and the Gemini API share the same underlying model and context window limits. AI Studio does not apply any additional truncation or summarization beyond the API limits. When a session nears the context limit, AI Studio surfaces a warning rather than silently truncating.

---

## 4. Meta (LLaMA 3.x)

### 4.1 Model-Level Architecture

LLaMA 3.1/3.2/3.3 (128K context) uses **Grouped Query Attention (GQA)** as the primary architectural KV cache optimization:
- LLaMA 3.1 70B: 8 KV heads instead of 64 query heads → 8× reduction in KV cache memory vs. standard MHA
- LLaMA 3.1 8B: Same GQA ratio

**No sliding window attention.** LLaMA 3 uses full attention across the 128K window. Meta's production inference uses FP8 quantization (quantizing from BF16) to reduce compute requirements, cutting memory roughly in half.

**KV cache memory at 128K:**
- LLaMA 3.1 70B: ~40 GB per user for 128K context (FP16)
- LLaMA 3.1 8B: ~16 GB for 128K context (FP16)
- GQA reduces this significantly; FP8 halves it again

Meta has published no documented summarization or compaction API for LLaMA. Context management is left to inference frameworks and application developers.

### 4.2 vLLM (Primary Production Inference Framework)

**PagedAttention:** vLLM's foundational innovation. KV cache is divided into fixed-size **blocks** (16 tokens by default). Blocks are stored in non-contiguous physical memory via a page-table indirection. Each request's KV blocks can be scattered across GPU RAM. This eliminates memory fragmentation (waste reduced to <4%).

**Prefix caching (Automatic Prefix Caching / APC):** vLLM hashes logical KV blocks by their content. Requests sharing identical prompt prefixes reuse physical blocks. Hash-based deduplication; hit rates of 60–85% achievable in agent loops, multi-tenant SaaS, and long-document Q&A. This is analogous to Anthropic's prompt caching but happens at the inference layer, not the API layer.

**KV cache eviction:** When physical memory is exhausted, vLLM evicts blocks with `reference_count == 0` using LRU (Least Recently Used) policy. **No content-aware eviction** — it does not look at attention scores to determine which blocks are semantically important.

**KV cache quantization:** vLLM supports `kv_cache_dtype="fp8_e4m3"` and `fp8_e5m2` on CUDA 11.8+/ROCm. FP16→FP8 reduces cache memory by 2× with minimal quality impact.

**PagedEviction (2025 research):** Block-wise eviction algorithm that uses attention scores to identify and remove low-importance blocks without modifying CUDA kernels. Not yet mainstream in production vLLM deployments but represents the direction of content-aware eviction.

### 4.3 llama.cpp

**Context management:** When `n_ctx` is exceeded, llama.cpp uses a **rolling window** strategy by default. The first half of non-kept tokens is discarded to make room. This is a hard truncation — no summarization.

**RoPE scaling for context extension:** llama.cpp supports RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) scaling techniques to extend effective context beyond training length:
- Linear scaling
- YaRN (Yet another RoPE extensioN): enabled via `--rope-scaling yarn --rope-scale 4 --yarn-orig-ctx 32768`
- LongRoPE
- These are **positional extrapolation techniques**, not compression. They allow the model to generalize its position encodings to longer sequences but with potential quality degradation.

**KV cache shifting:** The `llama_kv_cache::update` function updates RoPE frequencies of existing tokens when the window shifts, without re-processing them.

### 4.4 Ollama

Ollama wraps llama.cpp. Key parameter: `num_ctx` (default: 2,048 in Modelfile spec, 4,096 in practice). When cumulative token count exceeds `num_ctx`, Ollama **silently drops oldest messages** with no warning or error to the user or application. There is no rolling summary, no notification — just silent oldest-first truncation.

---

## 5. Mistral

### 5.1 Mistral 7B (Original, v0.1) — Sliding Window Attention

The original Mistral 7B used **Sliding Window Attention (SWA)** with window size 4,096 tokens and a rolling buffer:
- Each attention layer attends only to the previous 4,096 tokens (not the full sequence)
- Linear compute cost: O(seq_len × sliding_window) instead of O(seq_len²)
- With 32 decoder layers, theoretical attention span ≈ 4,096 × 32 = 131,072 tokens through information cascading across layers
- Fixed KV cache size: sliding_window tokens, using a rotating buffer → halved cache vs. naive full-attention at seq_len = 8,192

**SWA was removed in Mistral 7B v0.2:** The v0.2 update dropped sliding window attention in favor of a larger 32K context window with full attention and `rope_theta = 1e6`. This suggests the practical benefits of SWA did not outweigh the quality tradeoffs at larger scales.

### 5.2 Mistral Large 2 (123B, 128K context, July 2024)

Mistral Large 2 uses:
- **Full attention** (not SWA) — confirmed by the absence of SWA in its documentation
- **Grouped Query Attention (GQA):** 48 attention heads, 8 KV heads → 6× KV cache reduction vs. full MHA
- **Flash Attention** for inference speed optimization
- **128K context window**
- Designed for "single-node inference" (fits on one node at ~75GB with fp4 quantization)
- No application-level summarization or compaction API

**Mistral Large 3 (2025):** Returns to MoE architecture (675B total, 41B active parameters), 256K context window. No published details on context management mechanisms beyond the expanded window.

### 5.3 Mistral API — No Compaction

Mistral's API offers no documented thread management, truncation strategies, or compaction features comparable to OpenAI's Assistants API or Anthropic's Compaction API. Context limits produce errors; developers manage context externally.

---

## 6. Other Notable Approaches

### 6.1 Cohere Command R/R+ — RAG-First Architecture

Command R and Command R+ represent a different philosophy: rather than compressing context, **avoid needing to compress it** by externalizing memory into a retrieval system.

**Architecture:**
- 128K context window (104B parameters for Command R+)
- Trained with "grounded generation" capability: the model is specifically fine-tuned (via SFT + preference optimization) to generate responses from retrieved document snippets and produce inline citations pointing to sources
- **RAG is a first-class API feature:** The `/chat` endpoint accepts a `documents` array; the model handles citation extraction natively
- **Rerank API:** Retrieved chunks are reranked via a separate cross-encoder model before being passed to the LLM. This ensures only the most relevant passages populate the context window — an **external pre-filtering** step that reduces context need rather than compressing after the fact

**Context management philosophy:** Do not put the whole document in the context window. Retrieve the relevant passages, rerank them, and pass only those. For enterprise workflows with large corpora, this is more cost-effective than 1M-token windows.

### 6.2 xAI Grok (Grok 4.1 / Grok 4.1 Fast)

**Context windows:**
- Grok 4.1: 256K tokens
- Grok 4.1 Fast: 2,000,000 tokens (claimed)

**Context management:** xAI describes a "smart memory" system for long inputs that "intelligently compresses or prioritizes information in very long inputs." When a chat exceeds the context window, Grok uses a **sliding window design** — discarding oldest tokens to make room for recent exchanges. This is standard truncation behavior.

**Transparency level:** Low. xAI's public documentation on context management mechanisms is minimal compared to Anthropic or OpenAI. The "smart memory" description appears to be marketing language for standard sliding-window truncation with possible attention-score-based prioritization, but no technical paper or API documentation confirms this.

### 6.3 DeepSeek V2/V3/R1 — Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA)

While not a consumer-facing API provider in the same way, DeepSeek's MLA is the most technically significant architectural approach to KV cache compression in production and deserves attention as it is being adopted by other inference frameworks.

**How MLA works:**
- Instead of caching full-resolution K and V tensors (as in MHA or GQA), MLA stores a **compressed latent representation** and reconstructs usable K/V during attention computation via up-projection
- K and V are down-projected into a small low-rank matrix (the latent vector); this matrix is what gets cached
- Combined impact: KV cache reduced by 93.3% vs. DeepSeek 67B (MHA baseline); 5.76× throughput improvement
- DeepSeek-V2's MLA achieves GQA-equivalent or better performance with only 2.25 effective KV groups
- Combined with FP8: KV cache reduced from 135 GB to 8 GB for large contexts (17× reduction)

**This is a lossless architectural compression** — the latent representation faithfully reconstructs K/V within numerical precision, not an approximation. It is currently used in DeepSeek V2, V3, and R1.

## Cross-Provider Comparative Analysis

### Mechanism Taxonomy

| Provider | Mechanism | Type | Lossy? | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Anthropic API | Compaction API | LLM summarization | Yes | Token threshold (configurable, min 50K, default 150K) |
| Anthropic API | Prompt caching | KV cache reuse | No (lossless) | Developer-marked breakpoints |
| Claude.ai product | Auto-summarization | LLM summarization | Yes | Context window limit (opaque) |
| OpenAI Assistants | Thread truncation | Middle-of-context drop | Yes | `max_prompt_tokens`, automatic |
| OpenAI Responses | Compaction | Encrypted LLM summary | Yes | `compact_threshold` (min 1K tokens) |
| OpenAI o1/o3 | Reasoning budget | Thought-token budget control | No (it's a budget, not compression) | `reasoning_effort` parameter |
| OpenAI o3 | Encrypted reasoning items | Cross-turn state persistence | No | Per-turn, stateless mode |
| GPT-4o/4.1 | None (passes full context) | — | — | — |
| ChatGPT product | Silent truncation | Oldest-first drop | Yes | 32K product limit |
| Gemini API | Context caching (explicit) | KV cache reuse | No | Developer-created cache objects |
| Gemini 2.5+ API | Implicit caching | Automatic KV cache reuse | No | Automatic, min 1K–4K tokens |
| Gemini 1.5+ long ctx | Full processing + CP | Context parallelism (distributed) | No | Scaled by hardware |
| LLaMA 3 (vLLM) | PagedAttention + prefix caching | Block-level KV reuse | No | LRU eviction when full |
| llama.cpp | Rolling window | First-half discard | Yes | n_ctx exceeded |
| Ollama | Silent oldest-first drop | Truncation | Yes | num_ctx exceeded |
| Mistral 7B v0.1 | SWA | Architectural window limit | Partial (window is bounded) | Per-layer, always active |
| Mistral Large 2+ | Full attention + GQA | KV head reduction | No | Always active |
| Cohere Command R+ | External RAG + rerank | Pre-filtering before context | No | External retrieval pipeline |
| DeepSeek (MLA) | Low-rank KV projection | Architectural compression | Near-lossless | Always active |

### Key Distinctions

**Summarization vs. truncation vs. retrieval vs. architectural:**
- **Summarization (Anthropic, OpenAI Responses):** Lossy but graceful. LLM generates a forward-looking summary that preserves task state. Requires an LLM pass (added latency and cost). Best for agentic workflows.
- **Truncation (OpenAI Assistants, ChatGPT, llama.cpp, Ollama):** Lossy and brittle. Simple drop of oldest or middle content. Fast, zero extra cost, but breaks task continuity unpredictably.
- **KV cache reuse (Anthropic prompt caching, Gemini context caching):** Lossless, purely an efficiency optimization. Does not extend effective context; reduces per-request compute cost for repeated prefixes.
- **Architectural compression (DeepSeek MLA, GQA, SWA):** Model-level, always active, lossless to near-lossless. Not controllable at API level. Reduces the memory footprint of the KV cache rather than reducing the number of tokens visible to the model.
- **External retrieval (Cohere RAG):** Avoids the problem entirely by never putting irrelevant content in the context. Requires retrieval infrastructure and adds latency from a retrieval/rerank pipeline.

**Automatic vs. manual:**
- Fully automatic, opaque: ChatGPT product truncation, Ollama overflow, Claude.ai summarization, Gemini implicit caching
- Automatic with developer visibility: Anthropic Compaction API, OpenAI Assistants truncation, OpenAI Responses compaction
- Manual/explicit developer control: Anthropic prompt caching (breakpoints), Gemini explicit context caching, OpenAI Assistants `last_messages` strategy

**Transparency:**
- Highest: Anthropic Compaction API — returns human-readable summary block; developer sees exactly what was preserved
- Medium: OpenAI Responses compaction — returns encrypted item; developer knows it happened but cannot read it
- Low: OpenAI Assistants — no notification of what was dropped
- None: ChatGPT product, Ollama — silent truncation

**Latency and cost impact:**
- Summarization-based compaction (Anthropic, OpenAI Responses) adds an extra LLM pass when triggered — adds seconds to response latency at trigger points, but amortizes across subsequent turns
- Prefix/context caching (Anthropic, Gemini) reduces per-request latency by ~40–80% for cache-heavy workflows; the cache write adds a one-time 25% surcharge
- Context parallelism (Gemini at scale): Enables serving 1M-token contexts at all, at ~77 seconds prefill for 405B models across 128 GPUs — viable but expensive
- Architectural approaches (GQA, MLA): Reduce VRAM requirements 6–17×, enabling longer contexts to fit in memory at all; no per-request overhead

## What is Official vs. Inferred vs. From Community Analysis

**Officially documented:**
- Anthropic Compaction API: full parameter reference, default prompt, ZDR support — [platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/compaction](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/compaction)
- OpenAI Assistants truncation strategies: [platform.openai.com/docs/assistants/deep-dive](https://platform.openai.com/docs/assistants/deep-dive)
- OpenAI Responses compaction: [developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/compaction](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/compaction)
- OpenAI reasoning token mechanics: [platform.openai.com/docs/guides/reasoning](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/reasoning)
- Gemini context caching (explicit + implicit): [ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/caching](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/caching)
- vLLM PagedAttention + prefix caching: [docs.vllm.ai/en/stable/design/prefix_caching](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/stable/design/prefix_caching/)
- Mistral 7B SWA: arxiv 2310.06825

**Officially disclosed results, mechanisms withheld:**
- Gemini 1.5 long-context recall performance: published; architecture enabling it: not disclosed (arxiv 2403.05530)
- Google's context parallelism infrastructure: described in research paper (arxiv 2411.01783), not in product docs

**Inferred from behavior / community analysis:**
- ChatGPT product truncation at ~32K limit: inferred from user reports and the known gap between API (128K/1M) and product limits
- Anthropic Claude.ai's summarization on context limit: mentioned in Anthropic help docs as a feature but no technical specification published
- Grok's "smart memory" = sliding window truncation: inferred from description; no technical paper

**From research (applicable to production but not deployed at named providers):**
- DeepSeek MLA: fully published (arxiv 2405.04434); deployed at DeepSeek, not (yet) at major US providers
- PagedEviction, ChunkKV, token merging: research papers, not deployed at API providers in standard form

- [Gemini 1.5 technical report - arxiv 2403.05530](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.05530)

Research how major LLM providers specifically handle context compression and long-context management. I need concrete technical details about their mechanisms, not vague descriptions.

Cover these providers in depth:

## 1. Anthropic (Claude)
- Already documented: Compaction API (compact-2026-01-12), 150K token threshold, LLM-generated task-forward summary, `pause_after_compaction` hook
- What else is known? Any additional mechanisms beyond the Compaction API? How does Claude.ai (the product, not API) handle it differently from the API?
- Does Claude use any architectural techniques (e.g., sliding window, KV cache compression) beyond the summarization approach?

## 2. OpenAI (GPT-4o, o1, o3, GPT-4.1)
- How does ChatGPT handle context limits? Is there documented auto-summarization?
- What does the Responses API or Assistants API do with long threads? Thread truncation vs. summarization?
- Does GPT-4o have any built-in compression, or does it just truncate?
- What about the o1/o3 reasoning models — how do they handle their internal "thinking" tokens and context?
- Any documented approach to the 128K/1M context windows for GPT-4.1?

## 3. Google (Gemini)
- Gemini 1.5/2.0/2.5 has 1M–2M token context windows. What is their approach to managing this?
- Do they use any compression, or just rely on the long context window?
- What is their KV cache strategy at these scales?
- Any published research on their long-context architecture (e.g., ring attention, linear attention variants)?
- Gemini in Google AI Studio vs. Gemini API — any differences in context management?

## 4. Meta (LLaMA 3.x)
- LLaMA 3 has 128K context. What compression or memory management is built in at the model or inference level?
- Any published research from Meta on handling long contexts in production?
- What do major LLaMA inference frameworks (vLLM, llama.cpp, Ollama) do for context management?

## 5. Mistral
- Mistral Large/Small context window management
- Any sliding window attention or other architectural choices that affect compression?
- How do they handle context at their API level?

## 6. Any other notable approach
- Cohere Command R+ and their RAG-first approach to long context
- xAI Grok's approach
- Any frontier model with a notably different compression strategy

## What I need for each provider:
1. **Mechanism**: Exactly HOW compression/truncation/management works (algorithm level)
2. **Trigger**: When does it activate (token threshold, user control, automatic)?
3. **What is preserved vs. discarded**: Is there a selection strategy?
4. **Quality maintenance**: What do they do to prevent quality loss?
5. **Developer control**: Can developers control or customize it?
6. **Transparency**: Does the user/developer know when compression happened?

## Key distinctions to analyze:
- Summarization vs. truncation vs. retrieval vs. architectural solution
- Automatic vs. manual control
- Lossy vs. lossless approaches
- In-context vs. external memory approaches
- How each approach affects: latency, cost, quality, developer experience

Search for:
- OpenAI Assistants API thread management / truncation strategy
- "GPT-4 context compression" OR "ChatGPT long context management"
- Gemini long context 1M token architecture
- "LLaMA context management" vLLM KV cache
- site:arxiv.org "context compression" LLM 2024 2025
- Each provider's official API documentation on context/token management
- Any leaked system prompts or technical analyses of how each provider handles context

Be specific and technical. Cite sources. Distinguish between what is officially documented vs. inferred from behavior vs. from leaks/community analysis.

content.text:
I now have enough detailed information to write a comprehensive, technical report. Let me compile everything.

---

# LLM Context Compression and Long-Context Management: Technical Deep Dive

---

## 1. Anthropic (Claude)

### 1.1 Compaction API (Beta: `compact-2026-01-12`)

**Mechanism (officially documented):**
This is the primary developer-facing long-context management tool. It is a server-side summarization pipeline integrated into the Messages API. When triggered, Claude itself generates a human-readable (but task-forward) summary of prior conversation history. The summary is returned as a `compaction` block at the head of the assistant response.

**Trigger:** Input token count threshold. Default is 150,000 tokens. Configurable via the `trigger` parameter with `type: "input_tokens"` and a `value` field. Minimum configurable threshold is 50,000 tokens.

**Parameter structure:**
```json
{
  "context_management": {
    "edits": [{
      "type": "compact_20260112",
      "trigger": {"type": "input_tokens", "value": 150000},
      "pause_after_compaction": false,
      "instructions": null
    }]
  }
}
```

**What is preserved vs. discarded:** The model generates a summary wrapped in `<summary></summary>` tags. The default summarization prompt (revealed in official docs) is:
> "You have written a partial transcript for the initial task above. Please write a summary of the transcript. The purpose of this summary is to provide continuity so you can continue to make progress towards solving the task in a future context, where the raw history above may not be accessible and will be replaced with this summary. Write down anything that would be helpful, including the state, next steps, learnings etc."

All prior message blocks are then dropped server-side. Only the `compaction` block and any subsequent turns remain. This is **lossy** — the raw history is irrecoverably discarded on the next turn. Multiple compactions in a single session are additive: each new compaction block supersedes the previous one.

**Custom instructions:** The `instructions` parameter completely replaces the default prompt. Developers can scope it (e.g., "Focus on preserving code snippets and variable names").

**pause_after_compaction hook:** When `pause_after_compaction: true`, the API returns early with `stop_reason: "compaction"`, giving the developer the opportunity to inject additional content (messages, instructions) into the context before resuming. This allows hybrid strategies like preserving specific recent messages alongside the summary.

**Developer control and transparency:**
- The developer must explicitly opt in (beta header required)
- Compaction counter pattern supported: `n_compactions * trigger_threshold` estimates cumulative token spend
- The compaction block is returned as a visible, human-readable content block (not opaque)
- Supported on: claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-opus-4-6, claude-opus-4-7, claude-mythos-preview

**Quality maintenance:** The summary model is the same model handling the conversation (not a separate smaller summarizer). Anthropic's rationale, documented in the "Effective Context Engineering" engineering blog, is that quality degrades in very long contexts even within window — compaction improves focus, not just stays under limits.

### 1.2 Prompt Caching (separate from compaction)

This is not context compression — it is KV cache reuse across requests. Developers mark cache breakpoints with `cache_control: {type: "ephemeral"}`. The API stores the KV tensors (keys and values for every attention head and layer) up to that breakpoint and reuses them on requests with an identical prefix.

- **Cache lifetime:** 5 minutes (refreshed on each hit at no cost)
- **Minimum token thresholds:** 1,024 tokens for Sonnet/Haiku; up to 2,048–4,096 for Opus
- **Cost structure:** 25% surcharge on writes; 10% of normal input price on reads (~$0.50/M vs. $5/M)
- **Prefix matching:** Hash-based. Even a 1-token difference causes a complete cache miss

This is a **lossless** inference optimization, not a compression technique. It does not reduce tokens visible to the model; it reduces recomputation.

### 1.3 Claude.ai Product (vs. API)

Claude.ai uses a different mechanism from the API's compaction feature. According to Anthropic's help documentation, when a conversation approaches the context window limit in Claude.ai (200K tokens for Claude 3.5/4 Sonnet), the product automatically summarizes earlier messages to allow the conversation to continue. Crucially: **this does not count toward usage limits**, and users receive no explicit notification when it occurs. There is no `pause_after_compaction` equivalent for end users — the summarization is entirely opaque to them.

**The product's behavior is essentially forced summarization with no developer hooks, no custom instructions, and no transparency about what was preserved.**

### 1.4 Architectural Notes

No public evidence of Anthropic using sliding window attention, linear attention, or KV cache token eviction at the model architecture level for production Claude models. Anthropic's approach to very long contexts is: (1) large windows (200K), (2) prefix caching for reuse, (3) summarization-based compaction for overflow. There is no documented token-pruning or KV-merging happening at inference time in the way research papers describe.

---

## 2. OpenAI (GPT-4o, o1/o3, GPT-4.1)

### 2.1 ChatGPT Product (consumer)

ChatGPT has no documented summarization mechanism on the consumer side. GPT-4o in ChatGPT has a 32K token context window in the product interface, even though the underlying model supports up to 128K (GPT-4o) or 1M (GPT-4.1) via API. When a conversation exceeds the product's window, **older messages are silently truncated** — dropped from the context with no user notification. There is no automatic summarization in the consumer product; performance simply degrades. Users must manually start a new conversation or summarize context themselves.

### 2.2 Assistants API Thread Management

The Assistants API manages context through configurable truncation strategies on runs.

**Truncation strategies:**
- **`auto`** (default): Messages in the middle of the thread are dropped to fit `max_prompt_tokens`. OpenAI calls this "smart" truncation, but it is fundamentally middle-truncation — the system preserves the most recent messages and the earliest (often system) messages, dropping the middle.
- **`last_messages`**: Explicitly specify the number of most recent messages to include. Integer value required.

**Developer-level controls:**
- `max_prompt_tokens`: Maximum tokens allocated for the prompt portion of a run
- `max_completion_tokens`: Maximum tokens for the completion
- Thread limit: 100,000 messages per thread

**Transparency:** OpenAI does not surface which messages were dropped. There is no callback or event. The developer knows truncation occurred only by inspecting `usage` counts or by noticing missing context.

**No summarization in Assistants API** — this is pure truncation. No LLM-generated summary is injected.

### 2.3 Responses API Compaction (released February 2026)

OpenAI released server-side compaction for the Responses API, directly analogous to Anthropic's Compaction API.

**Mechanism:** Enable via `context_management` parameter with `compact_threshold` (minimum 1,000 tokens). When the rendered context crosses the threshold during a response, the server emits an **encrypted compaction item** in the response stream and prunes the prior context before continuing.

**Key distinction from Anthropic:** The compaction item is **opaque and encrypted** — "not intended to be human-interpretable." OpenAI describes it as carrying "key prior state and reasoning using fewer tokens" but does not disclose the summarization prompt or format. Compare with Anthropic's compaction block, which contains a human-readable summary.

**Standalone endpoint:** `/responses/compact` — accepts a full context window and returns a compacted version without a live request, useful for pre-compacting before long agent loops.

**Stateless patterns:**
- Input-array chaining: append output (including compaction items) to next input array; drop pre-compaction items to save latency
- `previous_response_id` chaining: pass only the new user message; the server resolves prior state

**ZDR support:** Compatible with `store=false`, maintaining Zero Data Retention compliance.

**Transparency:** The compaction item is returned to the developer (they must pass it back in subsequent requests), but its internal contents are not human-readable. The developer knows compaction occurred because the item appears in the output array.

### 2.4 GPT-4o and GPT-4.1: No Built-in Architectural Compression

GPT-4o (128K context) and GPT-4.1 (1M context) do not have any documented in-model compression at inference time. OpenAI has improved attention mechanisms for GPT-4.1 to achieve near-perfect "needle in a haystack" retrieval at all positions up to 1M tokens, but this is an accuracy improvement, not a compression mechanism. There is no sliding window or KV cache eviction in production at the application level — the full context is processed.

**Important caveat:** GPT-4.1 in the API supports 1M tokens, but ChatGPT as a product is capped at 32K context per conversation, demonstrating that the infrastructure choices for consumer products are deliberately more conservative than the underlying model capability.

### 2.5 o1/o3/o4 Reasoning Models

These models introduce a qualitatively different context management problem: internal chain-of-thought tokens.

**Reasoning token mechanics:**
- Reasoning tokens are generated during inference as an internal scratchpad but are **not returned in the standard API response**
- They are invisible to the developer in output, but **consume context window space and are billed as output tokens**
- Depending on complexity, they can range from hundreds to tens of thousands of tokens per request

**Context window accounting:** Input tokens + output tokens + reasoning tokens all count toward the context window total.

**Truncation for reasoning models (Responses API):** When using `auto` truncation strategy and the accumulated context (including reasoning items from prior turns) exceeds the model's context window, OpenAI drops input items from the **middle of the conversation** — same middle-truncation strategy as Assistants API.

**`reasoning_effort` parameter:** Controls the number of reasoning tokens the model allocates. Values: `none`, `minimal`, `low`, `medium`, `high`, `xhigh`. This is a budget knob, not a compression mechanism — setting it lower causes the model to think less, not compresses its thinking.

**Encrypted reasoning items (o3/o4-mini only):**
- The Responses API can return reasoning items with an `encrypted_content` field via `include: ["reasoning.encrypted_content"]`
- The encrypted blob is passed back on subsequent turns to give the model access to its prior reasoning state
- In stateless mode (`store=false`): the encrypted content is decrypted in-memory (never written to disk), used for generation, then discarded. New reasoning tokens are immediately re-encrypted and returned
- This allows reasoning state to persist across turns without OpenAI storing the raw reasoning traces, serving both privacy compliance and safety (reasoning traces are never directly observable)
- **This is not context compression** — it is reasoning state persistence. The encrypted content still occupies tokens in the context window

---

## 3. Google (Gemini 1.5/2.0/2.5)

### 3.1 Long Context Architecture: What's Actually Known

**From the Gemini 1.5 technical report (arxiv: 2403.05530):**
Gemini 1.5 Pro is a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformer. The paper claims "near-perfect recall (>99%) up to at least 10M tokens" and "100% recall at 530K tokens, 99.7% at 1M tokens." However, the paper is deliberately vague on implementation specifics — it describes the results but not the mechanisms that achieve them.

What is disclosed: "a series of significant architecture changes" enable long-context understanding. The report does not publicly specify whether this involves ring attention, linear attention, or sparse attention variants. Google treats the specific architectural mechanisms as proprietary.

**Inference infrastructure (from arxiv: 2411.01783 — "Context Parallelism for Scalable Million-Token Inference"):**
For serving long-context requests, Google uses **context parallelism (CP)** — distributing the KV cache and attention computation across multiple GPUs in a ring pattern:

- **Pass-KV algorithm:** Distributes key-value tensors across ranks in a ring. Each rank holds a chunk of the sequence; KV blocks circulate around the ring while each rank computes partial attention results. Most efficient for full-prefill scenarios.
- **Pass-Q algorithm:** Query embeddings circulate instead of KV. Preferred during decode and in scenarios with high prefix cache hit rates (when cached context far exceeds new tokens). The switching threshold is determined by: `T/(T+P) ≤ 2·(NKV/NH)` where T = new tokens, P = cached tokens, NKV = KV heads, NH = query heads.
- **Dynamic selection heuristic:** The system automatically selects between Pass-KV and Pass-Q based on the ratio of new to total tokens.
- **Scaling results:** Achieved 1M-token prefill with Llama3 405B in 77 seconds on 128 H100s (93% parallelization efficiency); 128K context in 3.8 seconds.

**This is a distributed inference strategy, not a compression mechanism.** The full context is processed without approximation — lossless, with near-linear latency scaling.

### 3.2 Context Caching

Google's context caching is the primary developer-facing long-context management tool.

**Explicit caching:**
- Developer sends a cacheable prefix (system prompt, large document, video, audio) to the `/cachedContents` API
- Google stores the prefix as a named cache object with a configurable TTL (default: 1 hour)
- Subsequent requests reference the cache object; the cached tokens are processed at 75% discount (90% on Gemini 2.5)
- Minimum token thresholds: 1,024 tokens (Gemini 2.5 Flash), 4,096 tokens (Gemini 2.5 Pro)
- The cached content functions strictly as a **prefix** to the prompt; it cannot appear mid-conversation
- Storage costs billed by TTL duration × cached token count

**Implicit caching (Gemini 2.5+, launched May 2025):**
- Enabled by default for all Gemini 2.5+ models — no developer action required
- The API automatically identifies common prefixes across requests and applies cache discounts
- 90% discount on Gemini 2.5 models (versus 75% on 2.0)
- Minimum: 2,048 tokens for Gemini 2.5 Pro, 1,024 for 2.5 Flash
- The `usage_metadata` field returns `cached_content_token_count` for transparency
- Unlike Anthropic's prompt caching, there is no developer-marked breakpoint — the infrastructure infers the common prefix automatically

**This is not context compression.** Like Anthropic's prompt caching, it reduces redundant computation (reusing KV tensors), not the amount of context the model processes.

### 3.3 No Application-Level Truncation or Summarization

Gemini's API does not offer any equivalent to Anthropic's Compaction API or OpenAI's Assistants API truncation. Google's explicit strategy is: **"skip the RAG workflows with a 2M context window"** — the context window is large enough that summarization and chunking are positioned as unnecessary. When a context exceeds the window, the API returns a context-too-long error; there is no automatic fallback or graceful truncation.

### 3.4 Google AI Studio vs. Gemini API

Google AI Studio (the consumer/developer UI) and the Gemini API share the same underlying model and context window limits. AI Studio does not apply any additional truncation or summarization beyond the API limits. When a session nears the context limit, AI Studio surfaces a warning rather than silently truncating.

---

## 4. Meta (LLaMA 3.x)

### 4.1 Model-Level Architecture

LLaMA 3.1/3.2/3.3 (128K context) uses **Grouped Query Attention (GQA)** as the primary architectural KV cache optimization:
- LLaMA 3.1 70B: 8 KV heads instead of 64 query heads → 8× reduction in KV cache memory vs. standard MHA
- LLaMA 3.1 8B: Same GQA ratio

**No sliding window attention.** LLaMA 3 uses full attention across the 128K window. Meta's production inference uses FP8 quantization (quantizing from BF16) to reduce compute requirements, cutting memory roughly in half.

**KV cache memory at 128K:**
- LLaMA 3.1 70B: ~40 GB per user for 128K context (FP16)
- LLaMA 3.1 8B: ~16 GB for 128K context (FP16)
- GQA reduces this significantly; FP8 halves it again

Meta has published no documented summarization or compaction API for LLaMA. Context management is left to inference frameworks and application developers.

### 4.2 vLLM (Primary Production Inference Framework)

**PagedAttention:** vLLM's foundational innovation. KV cache is divided into fixed-size **blocks** (16 tokens by default). Blocks are stored in non-contiguous physical memory via a page-table indirection. Each request's KV blocks can be scattered across GPU RAM. This eliminates memory fragmentation (waste reduced to <4%).

**Prefix caching (Automatic Prefix Caching / APC):** vLLM hashes logical KV blocks by their content. Requests sharing identical prompt prefixes reuse physical blocks. Hash-based deduplication; hit rates of 60–85% achievable in agent loops, multi-tenant SaaS, and long-document Q&A. This is analogous to Anthropic's prompt caching but happens at the inference layer, not the API layer.

**KV cache eviction:** When physical memory is exhausted, vLLM evicts blocks with `reference_count == 0` using LRU (Least Recently Used) policy. **No content-aware eviction** — it does not look at attention scores to determine which blocks are semantically important.

**KV cache quantization:** vLLM supports `kv_cache_dtype="fp8_e4m3"` and `fp8_e5m2` on CUDA 11.8+/ROCm. FP16→FP8 reduces cache memory by 2× with minimal quality impact.

**PagedEviction (2025 research):** Block-wise eviction algorithm that uses attention scores to identify and remove low-importance blocks without modifying CUDA kernels. Not yet mainstream in production vLLM deployments but represents the direction of content-aware eviction.

### 4.3 llama.cpp

**Context management:** When `n_ctx` is exceeded, llama.cpp uses a **rolling window** strategy by default. The first half of non-kept tokens is discarded to make room. This is a hard truncation — no summarization.

**RoPE scaling for context extension:** llama.cpp supports RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) scaling techniques to extend effective context beyond training length:
- Linear scaling
- YaRN (Yet another RoPE extensioN): enabled via `--rope-scaling yarn --rope-scale 4 --yarn-orig-ctx 32768`
- LongRoPE
- These are **positional extrapolation techniques**, not compression. They allow the model to generalize its position encodings to longer sequences but with potential quality degradation.

**KV cache shifting:** The `llama_kv_cache::update` function updates RoPE frequencies of existing tokens when the window shifts, without re-processing them.

### 4.4 Ollama

Ollama wraps llama.cpp. Key parameter: `num_ctx` (default: 2,048 in Modelfile spec, 4,096 in practice). When cumulative token count exceeds `num_ctx`, Ollama **silently drops oldest messages** with no warning or error to the user or application. There is no rolling summary, no notification — just silent oldest-first truncation.

---

## 5. Mistral

### 5.1 Mistral 7B (Original, v0.1) — Sliding Window Attention

The original Mistral 7B used **Sliding Window Attention (SWA)** with window size 4,096 tokens and a rolling buffer:
- Each attention layer attends only to the previous 4,096 tokens (not the full sequence)
- Linear compute cost: O(seq_len × sliding_window) instead of O(seq_len²)
- With 32 decoder layers, theoretical attention span ≈ 4,096 × 32 = 131,072 tokens through information cascading across layers
- Fixed KV cache size: sliding_window tokens, using a rotating buffer → halved cache vs. naive full-attention at seq_len = 8,192

**SWA was removed in Mistral 7B v0.2:** The v0.2 update dropped sliding window attention in favor of a larger 32K context window with full attention and `rope_theta = 1e6`. This suggests the practical benefits of SWA did not outweigh the quality tradeoffs at larger scales.

### 5.2 Mistral Large 2 (123B, 128K context, July 2024)

Mistral Large 2 uses:
- **Full attention** (not SWA) — confirmed by the absence of SWA in its documentation
- **Grouped Query Attention (GQA):** 48 attention heads, 8 KV heads → 6× KV cache reduction vs. full MHA
- **Flash Attention** for inference speed optimization
- **128K context window**
- Designed for "single-node inference" (fits on one node at ~75GB with fp4 quantization)
- No application-level summarization or compaction API

**Mistral Large 3 (2025):** Returns to MoE architecture (675B total, 41B active parameters), 256K context window. No published details on context management mechanisms beyond the expanded window.

### 5.3 Mistral API — No Compaction

Mistral's API offers no documented thread management, truncation strategies, or compaction features comparable to OpenAI's Assistants API or Anthropic's Compaction API. Context limits produce errors; developers manage context externally.

---

## 6. Other Notable Approaches

### 6.1 Cohere Command R/R+ — RAG-First Architecture

Command R and Command R+ represent a different philosophy: rather than compressing context, **avoid needing to compress it** by externalizing memory into a retrieval system.

**Architecture:**
- 128K context window (104B parameters for Command R+)
- Trained with "grounded generation" capability: the model is specifically fine-tuned (via SFT + preference optimization) to generate responses from retrieved document snippets and produce inline citations pointing to sources
- **RAG is a first-class API feature:** The `/chat` endpoint accepts a `documents` array; the model handles citation extraction natively
- **Rerank API:** Retrieved chunks are reranked via a separate cross-encoder model before being passed to the LLM. This ensures only the most relevant passages populate the context window — an **external pre-filtering** step that reduces context need rather than compressing after the fact

**Context management philosophy:** Do not put the whole document in the context window. Retrieve the relevant passages, rerank them, and pass only those. For enterprise workflows with large corpora, this is more cost-effective than 1M-token windows.

### 6.2 xAI Grok (Grok 4.1 / Grok 4.1 Fast)

**Context windows:**
- Grok 4.1: 256K tokens
- Grok 4.1 Fast: 2,000,000 tokens (claimed)

**Context management:** xAI describes a "smart memory" system for long inputs that "intelligently compresses or prioritizes information in very long inputs." When a chat exceeds the context window, Grok uses a **sliding window design** — discarding oldest tokens to make room for recent exchanges. This is standard truncation behavior.

**Transparency level:** Low. xAI's public documentation on context management mechanisms is minimal compared to Anthropic or OpenAI. The "smart memory" description appears to be marketing language for standard sliding-window truncation with possible attention-score-based prioritization, but no technical paper or API documentation confirms this.

### 6.3 DeepSeek V2/V3/R1 — Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA)

While not a consumer-facing API provider in the same way, DeepSeek's MLA is the most technically significant architectural approach to KV cache compression in production and deserves attention as it is being adopted by other inference frameworks.

**How MLA works:**
- Instead of caching full-resolution K and V tensors (as in MHA or GQA), MLA stores a **compressed latent representation** and reconstructs usable K/V during attention computation via up-projection
- K and V are down-projected into a small low-rank matrix (the latent vector); this matrix is what gets cached
- Combined impact: KV cache reduced by 93.3% vs. DeepSeek 67B (MHA baseline); 5.76× throughput improvement
- DeepSeek-V2's MLA achieves GQA-equivalent or better performance with only 2.25 effective KV groups
- Combined with FP8: KV cache reduced from 135 GB to 8 GB for large contexts (17× reduction)

**This is a lossless architectural compression** — the latent representation faithfully reconstructs K/V within numerical precision, not an approximation. It is currently used in DeepSeek V2, V3, and R1.

---

## Cross-Provider Comparative Analysis

### Mechanism Taxonomy

| Provider | Mechanism | Type | Lossy? | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Anthropic API | Compaction API | LLM summarization | Yes | Token threshold (configurable, min 50K, default 150K) |
| Anthropic API | Prompt caching | KV cache reuse | No (lossless) | Developer-marked breakpoints |
| Claude.ai product | Auto-summarization | LLM summarization | Yes | Context window limit (opaque) |
| OpenAI Assistants | Thread truncation | Middle-of-context drop | Yes | `max_prompt_tokens`, automatic |
| OpenAI Responses | Compaction | Encrypted LLM summary | Yes | `compact_threshold` (min 1K tokens) |
| OpenAI o1/o3 | Reasoning budget | Thought-token budget control | No (it's a budget, not compression) | `reasoning_effort` parameter |
| OpenAI o3 | Encrypted reasoning items | Cross-turn state persistence | No | Per-turn, stateless mode |
| GPT-4o/4.1 | None (passes full context) | — | — | — |
| ChatGPT product | Silent truncation | Oldest-first drop | Yes | 32K product limit |
| Gemini API | Context caching (explicit) | KV cache reuse | No | Developer-created cache objects |
| Gemini 2.5+ API | Implicit caching | Automatic KV cache reuse | No | Automatic, min 1K–4K tokens |
| Gemini 1.5+ long ctx | Full processing + CP | Context parallelism (distributed) | No | Scaled by hardware |
| LLaMA 3 (vLLM) | PagedAttention + prefix caching | Block-level KV reuse | No | LRU eviction when full |
| llama.cpp | Rolling window | First-half discard | Yes | n_ctx exceeded |
| Ollama | Silent oldest-first drop | Truncation | Yes | num_ctx exceeded |
| Mistral 7B v0.1 | SWA | Architectural window limit | Partial (window is bounded) | Per-layer, always active |
| Mistral Large 2+ | Full attention + GQA | KV head reduction | No | Always active |
| Cohere Command R+ | External RAG + rerank | Pre-filtering before context | No | External retrieval pipeline |
| DeepSeek (MLA) | Low-rank KV projection | Architectural compression | Near-lossless | Always active |

### Key Distinctions

**Summarization vs. truncation vs. retrieval vs. architectural:**
- **Summarization (Anthropic, OpenAI Responses):** Lossy but graceful. LLM generates a forward-looking summary that preserves task state. Requires an LLM pass (added latency and cost). Best for agentic workflows.
- **Truncation (OpenAI Assistants, ChatGPT, llama.cpp, Ollama):** Lossy and brittle. Simple drop of oldest or middle content. Fast, zero extra cost, but breaks task continuity unpredictably.
- **KV cache reuse (Anthropic prompt caching, Gemini context caching):** Lossless, purely an efficiency optimization. Does not extend effective context; reduces per-request compute cost for repeated prefixes.
- **Architectural compression (DeepSeek MLA, GQA, SWA):** Model-level, always active, lossless to near-lossless. Not controllable at API level. Reduces the memory footprint of the KV cache rather than reducing the number of tokens visible to the model.
- **External retrieval (Cohere RAG):** Avoids the problem entirely by never putting irrelevant content in the context. Requires retrieval infrastructure and adds latency from a retrieval/rerank pipeline.

**Automatic vs. manual:**
- Fully automatic, opaque: ChatGPT product truncation, Ollama overflow, Claude.ai summarization, Gemini implicit caching
- Automatic with developer visibility: Anthropic Compaction API, OpenAI Assistants truncation, OpenAI Responses compaction
- Manual/explicit developer control: Anthropic prompt caching (breakpoints), Gemini explicit context caching, OpenAI Assistants `last_messages` strategy

**Transparency:**
- Highest: Anthropic Compaction API — returns human-readable summary block; developer sees exactly what was preserved
- Medium: OpenAI Responses compaction — returns encrypted item; developer knows it happened but cannot read it
- Low: OpenAI Assistants — no notification of what was dropped
- None: ChatGPT product, Ollama — silent truncation

**Latency and cost impact:**
- Summarization-based compaction (Anthropic, OpenAI Responses) adds an extra LLM pass when triggered — adds seconds to response latency at trigger points, but amortizes across subsequent turns
- Prefix/context caching (Anthropic, Gemini) reduces per-request latency by ~40–80% for cache-heavy workflows; the cache write adds a one-time 25% surcharge
- Context parallelism (Gemini at scale): Enables serving 1M-token contexts at all, at ~77 seconds prefill for 405B models across 128 GPUs — viable but expensive
- Architectural approaches (GQA, MLA): Reduce VRAM requirements 6–17×, enabling longer contexts to fit in memory at all; no per-request overhead

---

## What is Official vs. Inferred vs. From Community Analysis

**Officially documented:**
- Anthropic Compaction API: full parameter reference, default prompt, ZDR support — [platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/compaction](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/compaction)
- OpenAI Assistants truncation strategies: [platform.openai.com/docs/assistants/deep-dive](https://platform.openai.com/docs/assistants/deep-dive)
- OpenAI Responses compaction: [developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/compaction](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/compaction)
- OpenAI reasoning token mechanics: [platform.openai.com/docs/guides/reasoning](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/reasoning)
- Gemini context caching (explicit + implicit): [ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/caching](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/caching)
- vLLM PagedAttention + prefix caching: [docs.vllm.ai/en/stable/design/prefix_caching](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/stable/design/prefix_caching/)
- Mistral 7B SWA: arxiv 2310.06825

**Officially disclosed results, mechanisms withheld:**
- Gemini 1.5 long-context recall performance: published; architecture enabling it: not disclosed (arxiv 2403.05530)
- Google's context parallelism infrastructure: described in research paper (arxiv 2411.01783), not in product docs

**Inferred from behavior / community analysis:**
- ChatGPT product truncation at ~32K limit: inferred from user reports and the known gap between API (128K/1M) and product limits
- Anthropic Claude.ai's summarization on context limit: mentioned in Anthropic help docs as a feature but no technical specification published
- Grok's "smart memory" = sliding window truncation: inferred from description; no technical paper

**From research (applicable to production but not deployed at named providers):**
- DeepSeek MLA: fully published (arxiv 2405.04434); deployed at DeepSeek, not (yet) at major US providers
- PagedEviction, ChunkKV, token merging: research papers, not deployed at API providers in standard form

---

Sources:
- [Compaction - Claude API Docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/compaction)
- [Compaction | OpenAI API](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/compaction)
- [Assistants API deep dive | OpenAI API](https://platform.openai.com/docs/assistants/deep-dive)
- [Reasoning models | OpenAI API](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/reasoning)
- [Better performance from reasoning models using the Responses API | OpenAI Cookbook](https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/responses_api/reasoning_items)
- [Context caching | Gemini API | Google AI for Developers](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/caching)
- [Gemini 2.5 Models now support implicit caching - Google Developers Blog](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/gemini-2-5-models-now-support-implicit-caching/)
- [Gemini 1.5 technical report - arxiv 2403.05530](https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.05530)
- [Context Parallelism for Scalable Million-Token Inference - arxiv 2411.01783](https://arxiv.org/html/2411.01783v1)
- [Quantized KV Cache - vLLM](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/features/quantization/quantized_kvcache/)
- [Automatic Prefix Caching - vLLM](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/stable/design/prefix_caching/)
- [Prompt caching - Claude API Docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-caching)
- [Introducing Llama 3.1 - Meta AI Blog](https://ai.meta.com/blog/meta-llama-3-1/)
- [Extending context size via RoPE scaling - llama.cpp GitHub](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions/1965)
- [Ollama num_ctx parameter - Hacker News](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=42833427)
- [Mistral 7B - arxiv 2310.06825](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06825)
- [Mistral Large 2 - Mistral AI](https://mistral.ai/news/mistral-large-2407)
- [Cohere's Command R+ Model](https://docs.cohere.com/docs/command-r-plus)
- [DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient MoE LLM - arxiv 2405.04434](https://arxiv.org/html/2405.04434v4)
- [TransMLA: Multi-Head Latent Attention Is All You Need - arxiv 2502.07864](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.07864)
- [How to use reasoning.encrypted_content with store=False - OpenAI Community](https://community.openai.com/t/how-to-use-reasoning-encrypted-content-with-store-false-stateless/1286934)
- [Grok AI Context Window - datastudios.org](https://www.datastudios.org/post/grok-ai-context-window-token-limits-and-memory-architecture-reply-ceilings-and-best-practices-f)
- [KV Cache Optimization Strategies - arxiv 2603.20397](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.20397v1)
- [Thread Truncation With New Assistants API - OpenAI Community](https://community.openai.com/t/thread-truncation-with-new-assistants-api/484648)
